coredata.dc:title: A rational synthesis strategy of multicolor carbon nanodots for efficient visual detection of NaNO2
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Gong, Xiao
Wu, Mengyi
Li, Jiurong
Zhang, Shiwei
Wu, Min
coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanodots (CDs) have seen unprecedented growth in recent years. Here, we report a facile synthesis strategy of tunable luminescent CDs via sulfur (S)-nitrogen (N) co-doping for visually detecting sodium nitrite (NaNO2). The prepared multicolor CDs have outstanding stability and good emission properties, and the emitted fluorescence of the CDs can be redshifted from 520 nm to 555 nm to 625 nm by introducing S and N elemental doping, reaching a redshift of wavelength greater than 100 nm. The narrowing of the energy gap caused by the increase in graphitic N and C = O levels might be the reason behind the adjustable luminescence mechanism of the CDs. This also suggests an increase in sp2 conjugated domains, which shifts the emission wavelength towards the red color. Besides, yellow CDs (Y-CDs) and red CDs (R-CDs) synthesized by S and N co-doping are extremely sensitive and specific in detecting NaNO2. Importantly, the detection of NaNO2 can be visualized by the color recognition software in the smart phone. We expect that this synthesis strategy will provide favorable technical support for the synthesis of emission redshift of high-performance CDs with special applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Luminescence
Carbon nanodots
Visual detection
NaNO2

Wu thanks the support by the Sichuan Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholars (2022JDJQ0049), and the Scientific and Technological Achievements Transformation Fund of West China Hospital, Sichuan University (Grant CGZH21002).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of multicolored CDs synthesis.
TEM image and particle size distribution image of G-CDs. (a, b, g), Y-CDs (c, d, h) and R-CDs (e, f, i).
XPS plots of the CDs. (a) XPS full spectrum. (b and c) C 1 s and O 1 s (G-CDs). (d, e and f) C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s (Y-CDs). (g, h and i) C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s (R-CDs).
Excitation and emission spectra of the CDs (a, d and g). The emission spectrum of the CDs (b, e and h) under various excitations. The fluorescence lifetimes of the CDs (c, f and i).
Schematic illustration of energy transfer of multicolor CDs.

(a) Comparing the bursting degree of Y-CDs caused by various substances. (b-c) Spectra of Y-CDs incorporating different concentrations of NO2 − and the relationship between fluorescence intensity and NO2 −. (d) Comparing the bursting degree of R-CDs caused by various substances. (e-f) Spectra of R-CDs incorporating different concentrations of NO2 − and relationship between fluorescence intensity and NO2 −.

(a) Diagram of test paper for sodium nitrite detection. (b) Photographs of Y-CDs test strips taken in natural light during the detection of sodium nitrite. (c) Photograph using UV light at 365 nm of a test paper for detecting sodium nitrite using R-CDs. (d) Linear plots were created to display the ratio of red value to blue value of the Y-CDs test paper and its relationship with the concentration of sodium nitrite. (e) Linear plot of the value of L (brightness) and the concentration of sodium nitrite for R-CDs detection test strips.

head.ce:title: A rational synthesis strategy of multicolor carbon nanodots for efficient visual detection of NaNO2

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Unit of Psychoradiology, Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences, Chengdu 610041, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cResearch Unit of Psychoradiology, Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences, Chengdu 610041, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon nanodots (CDs) have seen unprecedented growth in recent years. Here, we report a facile synthesis strategy of tunable luminescent CDs via sulfur (S)-nitrogen (N) co-doping for visually detecting sodium nitrite (NaNO2). The prepared multicolor CDs have outstanding stability and good emission properties, and the emitted fluorescence of the CDs can be redshifted from 520 nm to 555 nm to 625 nm by introducing S and N elemental doping, reaching a redshift of wavelength greater than 100 nm. The narrowing of the energy gap caused by the increase in graphitic N and C = O levels might be the reason behind the adjustable luminescence mechanism of the CDs. This also suggests an increase in sp2 conjugated domains, which shifts the emission wavelength towards the red color. Besides, yellow CDs (Y-CDs) and red CDs (R-CDs) synthesized by S and N co-doping are extremely sensitive and specific in detecting NaNO2. Importantly, the detection of NaNO2 can be visualized by the color recognition software in the smart phone. We expect that this synthesis strategy will provide favorable technical support for the synthesis of emission redshift of high-performance CDs with special applications.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanodots (CDs) are a new type of fluorescent nanomaterials with diameters usually less than 10 nm [1,2]. Due to their exceptional optical properties, CDs have garnered extensive attention in research [3,4]. There are two primary types of synthesis methods for CDs, namely, top-down and bottom-up [5,6]. Each type of synthesis method has corresponding advantages and characteristics, while the bottom-up synthesis method such as hydrothermal and microwave pyrolysis have the unique advantage of easy operation, and low cost [6,7]. Although the process of synthesizing CDs through hydrothermal reaction is relatively simple, the emission of CDs is limited to short wavelengths range of blue and green in most cases [8,9]. This drawback of CDs seriously hinders its applications in sensing, bio-imaging, and optoelectronics [10,11]. Up to now, synthesis of CDs with long wavelength and multicolor luminescence properties under single wavelength excitation is a popular research area [12,13].

So far, there are many synthetic pathways for multicolor luminescent CDs. Researchers have employed several standard techniques to control the light emission from CDs, including modifying the reaction parameters (temperature, time and solvent) [14], reaction concentration [15], and the ratio of raw materials [16], or multicolor CDs are prepared by complex and tedious means including post-treatment process (dialysis and column chromatography) [17]. The post-treatment process, in particular, has been significantly hindered by its limitations, including cumbersome and complex processes, and being time-consuming and labor-intensive. Nitrogen (N) doping is known to be a very effective means of modulating fluorescence properties and increasing the photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) because the addition of N atoms can change the internal electronic state of the carbon sites [18,19]. Furthermore, it is found that co-doping of two or more elements (e.g., co-doping of N and sulfur (S), co-doping of N and boron (B), co-doping of N, phosphorus (P), and S, etc. [20,21]) can achieve a synergistic effect to further improve and enhance electronic structure and photoluminescence (PL) properties of CDs [22]. In our previous work [23], by simultaneously doping N and S, the energy gap and structure of CDs were changed, which significantly enhanced the PLQY of CDs. Although there have been some reports on N and S co-doping, current studies on N and S co-doped CDs are limited to a single luminescence, and there are almost no reports on effective synthesis strategies related to the red-shifting of the luminescence wavelengths of CDs [24,25]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to construct a synthesis strategy to realize tunable luminescence of CDs through N and S co-doping.

Sodium nitrite (NaNO2) is widely used in the food processing industry. If people ingest NaNO2 in excess, it can lead to tissue hypoxia, methemoglobinopathies and increased risk of cancer [26]. Therefore, it is important to quickly and sensitively accurately determination of NaNO2 in the environment and food. [27,28] As an outstanding fluorescent probe in analytical tests, CDs now have a lot of potential applications. However, most of the methods utilizing CDs to detect NaNO2 mainly determine the concentration of NaNO2 only by judging changes in fluorescence intensity of the CDs [29,30]. Most fluorescence sensing strategies use a single fluorescent signal and are susceptible to factors such as instrument environment and probe concentration. Therefore, this method is not intuitive enough, and the narrow range of color variation of fluorescent probes does not facilitate testing in real environments, hindering its further application in visual analysis. With the explosion of smartphone data processing capabilities and devices in recent years, smartphone-assisted detection methods have been widely used in areas such as food and drugs [31–33]. Colorimetric image analysis methods using red, green and blue (RGB) components are widely applied for their visualization and high efficiency [34]. Portable image-based colorimetric methods allow for easy real-time on-site and quantitative analysis compared to traditional qualitative methods. Therefore, it becomes necessary to link it to the detection of NaNO2 through the use of color recognition software in smartphones.

In this work, we chose phthalic acid with a benzene ring structure as a precursor, and 2,5-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid as a source of N and S to prepare multicolor CDs. Simple co-doping of N and S produced multicolored luminous CDs that ranged from green to red without the need for laborious post-processing procedures. The optimal fluorescence emission of the manufactured trichromatic CDs was seen at 520, 555, and 625 nm, and the fluorescence lifetimes at 460 nm excitation were 6.13, 3.58 and 0.92 ns, respectively. By optimizing the ratio of reaction solvents, a wavelength redshift of nearly 105 nm was achieved. Interestingly, yellow CDs (Y-CDs) and red CDs (R-CDs) prepared by co-doping N and S exhibited great sensitivity and selectivity for detection of NaNO2. More importantly, the visualization of NaNO2 detection can be realized by the color recognition software in the smart phone. This method offers a tool for NaNO2 visualization and detection based on the smartphone platform with simple operation and good portability, which has a good application prospect in the field of sensing and analysis.

In this work, phthalic acid, 2,5-Diaminobenzenesulfonic acid, 2-Aminobenzenesulfonic acid, zinc chloride (ZnCl2), p-phenylenediamine, ascorbic acid, dopamine hydrochloride, glucose, manganese (II) chloride tetrahydrate (MnCl2 4H2O), sodium sulfide (Na2S), odium bromide (NaBr), sodium nitrite (NaNO2), ammonium chloride (NH4Cl), potassium iodide (KI), calcium chloride (CaCl2), sodium fluoride (NaF), copper (II) chloride dihydrate (CuCl2 2H2O), were supplied by Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd., China.

By using a JEM-2100F TEM (JEOL, Japan), transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) pictures were captured. A D8 Advance diffractometer (Bruker, Germany, Kα= 1.5406 A) was used to record X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra. A Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Nicolet 6700, Thermo Electron, USA) was used to record the carbon dots FT-IR spectra. Using a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (USA), the samples X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were examined. Using a time-resolved fluorescence spectrometer (FL3–22, Jobin-Yvon, USA), the carbon dots spectra were examined. A UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV2600, Shimadzu, Tokyo, Japan) was used to measure the absorption spectra of carbon dots. All images under visible and UV light were taken by a smartphone (iPhone 13) and with a 12 megapixel camera.

Green CDs (G-CDs): Briefly, 0.1 g of phthalic acid was taken as precursor and 5 mL of ethanol and 5 mL of deionized water were mixed as reaction solvent which has no N and S elements involved. The solution was ultrasonicated for 20 min to ensure that the mixture was completely dissolved. The solution was ultrasonicated for 20 min to ensure complete dissolution, then transferred to a 25 mL autoclave, and reacted in an oven at 180 °C for 12 h. The reacted solution was taken for further characterization.
Y-CDs: the synthesis of Y-CDs is similar to G-CDs, except that 0.1 g of 2,5-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid is added as a source of nitrogen and sulfur.
R-CDs: R-CDs are synthesized like Y-CDs, using deionized water as the reaction solvent.
NCDs-1: Synthesized in the same way as Y-CDs, except that the doping source was replaced by p-phenylenediamine.
NCDs-2: Synthesized in the same way as R-CDs except that the dopant source was p-phenylenediamine.
N, S-CDs-1: Synthesis was identical to Y-CDs except that the dopant source has been changed to 2-aminobenzenesulfonic acid.
N, S-CDs-2: Synthesis was identical to R-CDs except that the dopant source has been changed to 2-aminobenzenesulfonic acid.
G-CDs-1: Briefly, the synthesis process is the same as for G-CDs, with the only variation being the use of deionized water as the reaction solvent.
G-CDs-2: The synthesis process is the same as for G-CDs, with the only variation being the use of ethanol as the reaction solvent.
CDs-1: The reaction conditions were identical to those of the R-CDs, except that the reactants were not supplemented with phthalic acid.
CDs-2: Briefly, ethanol was used as the reaction solvent without the addition of phthalic acid, and the conditions of this reaction were the same as for R-CDs.
CDs-3: The reaction conditions were identical to those of the Y-CDs, except that the reactants were not supplemented with phthalic acid.

The fluorescence spectrum of the mixed solution was recorded using a spectrophotometer after mixing 100 µL of Y-CDs solution with 5 mL of deionized water and different amounts of NaNO2 aqueous solution, resulting in final NaNO2 concentrations ranging from 0 µM to 400 µM. The detection of sodium nitrite by R-CDs was the same as that of sodium nitrite by Y-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-assisted detection of NaNO2 using test paper
ce:section.ce:para: In order to visualize the detection of NaNO2, non-fluorescent filter paper was first cut into 1 cm Χ 3 cm as test paper for the assay. A series of Y-CDs and R-CDs solutions with varying NaNO2 concentrations were prepared. The test paper was immersed in the CDs solutions for 5 min, then removed and dried at room temperature. For the Y-CDs test papers, we chose to take pictures with a smartphone at a fixed position and angle under daylight. For the R-CDs, we placed a UV lamp inside the dark box and also took photos with a smartphone at a fixed position and angle. Finally, the finished photos were imported into the color desk of the smartphone for the analysis of the color parameters of the images.

The synthesis route of tricolor luminescent CDs is illustrated in Scheme 1 . Luminescent CDs with green, yellow, and red emissions were successfully prepared via using phthalic acid as a precursor material, adding 2,5-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid as an N and S source. The synthesized multicolor CDs all showed excellent dispersibility and good water solubility. Subsequently, TEM was applied to observe and analyze the microscopic morphology and structure of the multicolor CDs. From the low-resolution TEM in Fig. 1 a, 1c and 1e, it is evident that all the three prepared CDs have been well dispersed and no agglomeration occurs. As illustrated in Fig. 1g, 1h and 1i, the average particle sizes of G-CDs, Y-CDs and R-CDs were near 1.98, 2.94 and 3.81 nm. Compared to G-CDs without N and S co-doping, the particle size of N and S co-doped CDs increased significantly, which may be due to the doping of heteroatoms, leading to anomalous changes in the lattice structure of CDs. Since quantum size effects play a key role in regulating optical properties, an increase in particle size can cause the emission wavelength of the CDs to redshift [35,36]. As depicted in the HR-TEM Fig. 1b, 1d and 1f, the distinct lattice stripes of CDs can be observed, with all CDs having a consistent lattice spacing of about 0.21 nm, matching the (100) diffraction surface of graphitic carbon [37]. This indicates that the synthesized trichromatic emission CDs exhibit high-quality graphitized crystallinity.

The CDs were further characterized by XRD to determine their graphitic structure, as illustrated in Figure S1a. A broad diffraction peak at approximately 24.6° was observed in all three types of CDs, which suggests the presence of the graphitic structure of the (002) plane. This peak may be attributed to the graphitic nature of the CDs [38]. In addition, the functional groups present on the surface of CDs were analyzed using FT-IR. As indicated in Figure S1b, the telescopic vibrations of G-CDs at 3200–3500 cm−1 are linked to OH, while the vibrations of Y-CDs and R-CDs may be linked to OH/NH [39]. Since S and N-co-doped Y-CDs and R-CDs contain two hydrophilic groups, amino and hydroxyl, they are well soluble in water [40]. The stretching vibrations at 2990, 1090 and 1710 cm−1 correspond to CH, CO, and C = O, respectively in the three CDs [41,42]. The stretching vibrations near 1400 and 610 cm−1 in the infrared spectra of Y-CDs and R-CDs correspond to CN and C—S, respectively [43,44]. This is also well supported by the fact that the elements S and N were successfully doped, which shows that the introduction of new groups can indeed cause a change in the emission wavelength. Additionally, the FTIR spectra of all three CDs show a peak near 1510 cm−1, which is likely attributed to the C = C group stretching vibration. This suggests that an aryl ring skeleton may have been formed during the synthesis [45]. XPS technology can be utilized to analyze the elemental compositions of trichromatic CDs and the possible groups on the surfaces. As demonstrated by the full-scan XPS spectra in Fig. 2 a, the G-CDs consist mainly of C and O elements, comprising 76.16 % and 23.84 % of the composition, respectively. The Y-CDs and R-CDs are primarily made of C, N, O, and S elements, which account for approximately 59.27 %, 7.52 %, 28.12 %, and 5.09 %, respectively. To investigate the possible mode of chemical bonding between the elements, fine fitting of high-resolution XPS spectra of the elements was carried out. According to Fig. 2b, 2d, and 2g, the C 1 s spectra exhibit three major energy peaks in the three CDs samples, which are about 284.8, 286.1, and 288.2 eV, respectively, corresponding to the chemically bonded forms of CC/C = C, CO and C = O, respectively [46]. It is obvious that the G-CDs do not contain the N element, while as indicated in Fig. 2e and 2h, the characteristic peaks near 398.2, 399.2, and 400.3 eV of the N 1 s spectra of Y-CDs and R-CDs show peaks from three distinct nitrogen atoms: pyridine N, pyrrole N and graphite N [12]. The change in luminescence emission of Y-CDs compared to G-CDs may be attributed to the doping of N elements and the increase in graphite N content [47]. It is worth noting that the graphitic N content in R-CDs and Y-CDs is close to each other, which suggests that graphite N content is not the main cause of the further redshift of the emission wavelength from Y-CDs to R-CDs. To explore other factors leading to the PL redshift, we analyzed the O 1 s peaks of the three sample CDs. As indicated in Fig. 2c, 2f and 2i, two separate peaks at 531.5 eV and 532.7 eV correspond to the electronic binding energies of C = O and CO [48]. The R-CDs have the highest C = O content, followed by Y-CDs, and G-CDs have the lowest C = O content. This is strong evidence for the formation of carboxyl groups and increased oxidation, and the carboxyl groups attached to the sp2 hybridized carbon cause obvious local distortion. This in turn narrows the energy gap and triggers the fluorescence redshift. Therefore, the increase in C = O content is the key factor leading to further redshift of Y-CDs emission wavelength [49]. Figure S2 demonstrates the S 2p spectra of Y-CDs and R-CDs show two peaks at 164.5 and 165.6 eV, corresponding to C—S and S = O [50].

To explore the optical properties of trichromatic CDs, we dispersed these CDs in water and characterized them in detail by absorption spectra as well as fluorescence spectra. Figure S3a shows that the tricolor CDs exhibit absorption at approximately 290 nm, arising from the π-π* transitions of the aromatic C = C bonds, additionally, peaks observed between 300 nm and 400 nm are caused by n-π* transitions of the surface functional group C = O bonds [51]. R-CDs exhibit additional absorption bands at 420 nm and 530 nm, unlike G-CDs and Y-CDs. This is attributed to surface-conjugated groups on the core edges that further narrow the band gap of the fluorescent material [52]. And it can be obtained from the excitation and absorption spectra that the absorption and excitation peaks of R-CDs are very close to each other, which indicates that the fluorescence of R-CDs is mainly affected by the surface state. As illustrated in Fig. 3 a, 3d and 3g, at optimum excitation wavelength, the CDs showed green, yellow and red fluorescence, respectively, and the optimal fluorescence emission peaks are centered at about 520, 555 and 625 nm, respectively. After deeply exploring the optical properties of the tricolor CDs at varying excitation wavelengths, we found that in Fig. 3b and 3h, fluorescence spectra of G-CDs and R-CDs do not change significantly at different excitation wavelengths. This phenomenon may originate from the fact that they possess relatively homogeneous sizes and stable surface states, resulting in their optical properties being less affected by changes in excitation wavelengths. Whereas, as shown in Fig. 3e, Y-CDs exhibited a clear excitation dependence, and the correlation between the tunable photoluminescence behavior and the excitation wavelength mainly originates from multiple launch centers that exist for CDs [53]. Detailed optical characterization was carried out for different concentrations of CDs. From Figures S3b and S3c, the emission wavelengths of G-CDs and Y-CDs remained constant regardless of concentration. However, the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs and Y-CDs was enhanced with higher concentrations. This could be caused by the enhanced fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) effect impacting the intensity of fluorescence emission. Additionally, as depicted in Figure S3d, the emission behavior of synthetic R-CDs, like most of the known CDs, is affected by concentration. At a fixed excitation wavelength, when the concentration of R-CDs ranged from 0.5 to 30 mg/mL, the fluorescent peak redshifted from 605 to 625 nm. This is because with increasing CDs concentration, the distance between the particles decreases, leading to enhanced inter-particle interactions and triggering the self-absorption phenomenon, which leads to a redshift in PL emission [54]. Finally, Fig. 3c, 3f, and 3i display time-resolved fluorescence decay curves to evaluate and better understand the optical properties. The fluorescence lifetimes of G-CDs, Y-CDs and R-CDs can be fitted by the biexponential formula, and the average fluorescence lifetimes for G-CDs, Y-CDs and R-CDs are about 6.13, 3.58, and 0.92 ns, respectively. This short and decreasing fluorescence lifetime suggests that the core state predominantly governs the recombination pathway, and the rate of decay speeds up as the sp2 structural domain increases [55]. This is also very consistent with the fluorescence lifetimes reported in the previously published literature on multicolor CDs [37]. These results demonstrate the excellent optical properties of our synthesized CDs.

The key role of N and S co-doping was investigated in the synthesis of tricolor CDs. We selected some different doping sources as control experimental groups. Firstly, we selected p-phenylenediamine as the dopant source, which has a structure similar to that of 2,5-Diaminobenzenesulfonic acid but does not contain S, and named the synthesized CDs as NCDs-1 and NCDs-2, respectively. The aqueous solution optical properties of NCDs-1 and NCDs-2 were investigated, which are illustrated in Figure S4. It is obvious from the PL emission spectra that the experimental results do not agree with the prepared trichromatic CDs if p-phenylenediamine, which does not contain S, is chosen as the doping source. This is a good illustration of the fact that N doping alone cannot achieve multicolor luminescence from green to yellow to red in this system. In the reaction process, the co-doping of S and N leads to the formation of groups such as S = O and -NH2. The formation of S = O and -NH2 can be a major factor in the formation of graphite. These groups cause structural defects at the edges of the graphite. The luminescent properties of CDs are closely associated with localized conjugated structures that contain surface defects [56]. Sulfur lattice doping can create an extra energy level between the n and π* levels of carbon, thus triggering multiple electronic transition paths during absorption and luminescence [44]. The introduction of S can also provide excited state electrons, which tend to emit at longer wavelengths through inter-band crossing [57]. The increase of the -NH2 group can reduce the energy gap by boosting the CDs (HOMO) energy levels [58,59], thus the co-doping of N and S realizes the redshift of PL emission. Besides, we chose 2-Aminobenzenesulfonic acid, which has the same type of 2,5-Diaminobenzenesulfonic acid moiety but contains a different number of amino groups, as the doping source. We named the synthesized CDs as N, S-CDs-1 and N, S-CDs-2. When they were dispersed in water, the fluorescent peak of N, S-CDs-1 and N, S-CDs-2 were 510 and 538 nm, respectively. N, S-CDs-1 showed an obvious excitation-dependent phenomenon, while N, S-CDs-2 exhibited good excitation-independent properties, as demonstrated in Figure S5. Specifically, it follows that the synthesized CDs did not achieve the red wavelength emission if 2-Aminobenzenesulfonic acid, which has a smaller number of amino groups, is replaced as the doping source of S and N. It is possible that when 2,5-Diaminobenzenesulfonic acid with more amino groups is elected as the doping source, in the synthesized CDs after the reaction, the content of graphite N is higher, which leads to an increase in the emission wavelength. The results of the above control experiments, it is clear that our use of 2,5-Diaminobenzenesulfonic acid as a doping source of N and S elements to construct multicolor luminescent CDs is a reasonable choice.

Additionally, we carried out several sets of control experiments in order to better investigate the doping affectivity of N and S in our experiments. First, regarding the experimental control group of G-CDs, the experiments were prepared in deionized water and anhydrous ethanol, respectively, and all experimental conditions were consistent with the synthesis process of G-CDs. As depicted in Figure S6, in comparison to the correlation spectrum of G-CDs, the spectra of the control experiments produced significant changes in fluorescence emission, presenting 530 nm and 478 nm emission in deionized water and anhydrous ethanol, respectively. Moreover, the PL of the control experiments also showed a clear excitation dependence under different excitations. This may be due to the fact that the mixture of solvents helps in the synthesis of the reaction and increases the carbonation dehydration condensation of the reaction. The experiments were labeled as G-CDs-1 and G-CDs-2. Second, experiments on Y-CDs and R-CDs verified the phenomenon of 2,5-Diaminobenzenesulfonic acid in deionized water, anhydrous ethanol, and deionized water and anhydrous ethanol mixed together as a solvent, respectively. The experiments were labeled CDs-1, CDs-2 and CDs-3. As shown in Figure S7, the spectra of the control experiments produced significant changes in the fluorescence emission peak positions compared to the correlation spectrum of Y-CDs and R-CDs. In deionized water, the PL of CDs-1 showed dual fluorescence peaks at 480 nm and 580 nm independent of excitation. Similarly, when in anhydrous ethanol, the PL of CDs-2, although presenting a single fluorescent peak at 455 nm, also had a shoulder fluorescent peak at about 550 nm. By increasing the wavelength of excitation, the intensity of the fluorescence peak at 550 nm became higher, while the main emission at 455 nm gradually weakened, indicating dual emission centers in the prepared CDs-2. Similarly, when in a deionized water and anhydrous ethanol mixture, the PL emission center of CDs-3 was located at about 540 nm, but the half-height width was significantly smaller than that of the former two CDs (CDs-1 and CDs-2). At low the wavelength of excitation, a shoulder peak also appeared at about 450 nm, but as the wavelength of excitation increased, the PL intensity gradually diminished and there was only one major emission peak, which also showed excitation-independent characteristics. These results indicate that the mixed solvents help to facilitate the synthesis process and increase the carbonation of the reaction. In all the control experiments described above, the synthesized CDs did not have bright luminescence and the fluorescence was dark. Also, the importance of the carboxyl component in promoting fluorescence enhancement and redshift in the reaction was indirectly demonstrated.

In order to probe more deeply into the possible emission mechanisms of multicolor CDs, we can calculate the energy band gap of CD using the Eq. [60]:
(1)
E
g
opt
= 1240 /
λ edge
λedge represents the beginning of the initial exciton absorption peak in the long wavelength orientation. To visualize the energy gap changes and CDs structure more intuitively, as shown in Fig. 4 . We propose potential structural models and corresponding energy levels. In order of G-CDs, Y-CDs and R-CDs, the calculated band gaps are 2.38, 2.23 and 1.98 eV, respectively. As the band gap decreases, the fluorescent peak shifts from 520 to 555 and then to 625 nm. And the sizes are increased from 1.98 nm to 2.94 nm to 3.81 nm, which further demonstrates the obvious size dependence of the band gap energy. TEM observations revealed a noticeable redshift in the emission wavelength as the particle size of the tricolor CDs increased. This indicates that the quantum size effect may play a significant role in controlling the fluorescence of carbon dots [61]. The wavelength emission from green to yellow was achieved by co-doping of S and N, and it is obvious that the increase of graphitized N can effectively reduce the bandgap of CDs because it can form interstitial states in the original gap. Graphite N content affects the size domains or conjugation lengths of sp2 leading to the fluorescence redshift [62]. From the measured XPS results, the C = O content significantly increases with the red-shift of the fluorescence wavelength, indicating that the C = O content contributes significantly to the redshift of the wavelength. It is possible that the participation of C = O in sp2 hybridization leads to the increase of the π-conjugated structure, and another factor is that the C = O group is strongly electron-absorbing due to the presence of oxygen, is capable of lowering the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy level of CDs, thus reducing the energy gap [63]. Summarizing the above studies, we suggest that the tunable luminescence mechanism of CDs may be due to the reduction in the energy gap due to the rise in graphitic N and C = O content, which also suggests an increase in sp2 conjugated domains, which further results in the red shift of the emitted wavelength.

Since our synthesized CDs have outstanding optical properties and usually N and S co-doped CDs contain abundant functional groups, Y-CDs and R-CDs have promising applications in the area of sensing. To construct excellent optical sensing systems, it is important to further investigate the optical stabilization properties of Y-CDs and R-CDs under different conditions. As shown in Figure S8, the PL intensity of Y-CDs and R-CDs was slightly reduced after storing them at room temperature for 7 days. Importantly, the PL intensities of Y-CDs and R-CDs exhibited only slight fluctuations in the pH range of 3.0–11.0, indicating that the luminescent properties of CDs are stable in physiological environments. And no significant change in the fluorescence intensity of Y-CDs and R-CDs was observed in the temperature range of 20 to 90 °C, indicating that the fluorescence intensity was not affected by temperature. Subsequently, we evaluated the optical stability of CDs in salt solutions by measuring the fluorescence intensities of Y-CDs and R-CDs in different concentrations of NaCl solutions. The experimental results showed that the effect of high NaCl concentration on the fluorescence intensity was very small and negligible even at 5 M NaCl solution concentration. The above experiments and results confirm that CDs have good fluorescence stability and can be expected to build fluorescent sensing systems. To study the detection ability of Y-CDs and R-CDs in more detail, a series of substances were detected using the synthesized Y-CDs and R-CDs as depicted in Fig. 5 a and 5d. Interestingly, in addition to the G-CDs, the synthesized Y-CDs and R-CDs showed good sensitivity for the detection of NaNO2 (Figure S9a). More importantly, as shown in Figure S9b, even after leaving the CDs at room temperature for 10 days, sodium nitrite was still able to achieve fluorescence bursting of the CDs, indicating that the detection performance of Y-CDs and R-CDs against sodium nitrite does not diminish over time. We speculate that the rich functional groups on the surface of CDs are mainly due to the introduction of more functional groups on the surface of CDs after doping, such as -SH and -NH2, which can combine with various detection substances to make the CDs undergo fluorescence changes, and therefore, the rich functional groups on the surface of CDs are the basis of realizing the high sensitivity sensing and detection of hazardous substances [64].

Considering the presence of potential other metal ions in the actual samples that may affect the fluorescence detection of sodium nitrite, the specificity and immunity of CDs to sodium nitrite were tested. Figure S10 and Figure S11 show the fluorescence pictures and fluorescence intensity when different detection substances were added to Y-CDs and R-CDs, and the fluorescence of Y-CDs and R-CDs could only be quenched by NaNO2. Subsequently, the anti-interference performance of CDs was investigated, as shown in Figure S12. When other metal ions and anions as well as a variety of organic substances were present, there was almost no effect on the detection of sodium nitrite, which indicated that Y-CDs and R-CDs had a high degree of immunity to interference for the detection of sodium nitrite. Therefore, Y-CDs and R-CDs are ideal fluorescent materials for the effective determination of sodium nitrite in complex samples. Fig. 5b and 5e examine in detail the addition of different concentrations of NaNO2 to the CDs solution and show the PL spectra. And the figure displays the fluorescence intensity curve at various NO2 − concentrations. From Fig. 5c and 5f, it demonstrates a strong linear relationship between Y-CDs and R-CDs for detecting low concentrations of NaNO2. The calculation of the detection limit can be performed according to the following formula [65]:
(2)
LOD = 3
Δ
S / b

Where ΔS denotes the standard deviation of the PL intensity of CDs without sodium nitrite and b represents the slope of the linear equation. The LOD for Y-CDs and R-CDs were calculated as 0.692 µM and 1.730 µM, respectively, with good linearity (R1 2= 0.9873, R2 2= 0.9935).

We further explored the bursting mechanism of Y-CDs and R-CDs for NaNO2 detection. Since the precursors for the synthesis of Y-CDs and R-CDs contain reactants such as acids, both CDs solutions are acidic, and thus in acidic media, NO2 − and CDs or other small molecules with aromatic amino groups can undergo diazotization-specific reactions and a possible diagram of the diazotization reaction is shown in Figure S13. As demonstrated in Figure S14a, we first investigated the fluorescence lifetime changes of Y-CDs before and after adding of NaNO2. After adding NaNO2 to Y-CDs, the fluorescence lifetime was significantly reduced from 3.58 ns to 2.89 ns. This suggests that during dynamic quenching, NO2 − preferentially collides with CDs, leading to fluorescence shutdown via photoinduced electron transfer (PET) [66]. Moreover, PET induced by nitrosylation and diazotization reactions on the surface of CDs also contributes to the fluorescence shutdown of CDs [67]. The absorption spectrum of Y-CDs were further tested. The absorption spectra of the Y-CDs solution changed significantly after the addition of NO2 − (Figure S14b). Upon addition of NO2 − the absorption of Y-CDs was altered, suggesting that a new non-fluorescent complex may have been formed [68]. It is presumed that the diazotization reaction between sodium nitrite and Y-CDs generates colored substances. These formed colored substances can selectively quench the fluorescence of Y-CDs due to their spectral overlap [26]. According to Figure S14c, there is no a clear region of overlap between the peaks of the excitation and emission spectra of Y-CDs and the absorption spectrum of sodium nitrite, thus ruling out the effect of internal filtering effect (IFE) on the fluorescence burst of Y-CDs [27]. Combined with the characterization of fluorescence lifetime and absorption, it is concluded that the fluorescence burst of Y-CDs is not only affected by dynamic bursting and photoinduced electron transfer, but also the complexes generated by the diazotization reaction have a great influence on the fluorescence burst.

The mechanism of fluorescence bursting of R-CDs was subsequently explored, in Figure S14d, the fluorescence lifetimes of the R-CDs remained unchanged upon the addition of sodium nitrite. The lack of change in fluorescence lifetime indicates that no electron transfer between R-CDs and sodium nitrite occurred, ruling out the effects of dynamic bursting and PET on R-CDs. Combining the spectra shown in Figures S14e and S14f, there is no overlap between the absorption peaks of sodium nitrite and the excitation emission peaks of R-CD, which suggests that the burst is not caused by the IFE process. So the fluorescence burst of R-CDs is hypothesized to be a static burst process. To summarize, the possible mechanism of fluorescence burst of CDs detecting sodium nitrite is shown schematically in Figure S15.

We then compared a series of related works on the detection of sodium nitrite (Table S1), which can be obtained that previous methods of detecting sodium nitrite with CDs are basically limited to fluorescence intensity or colorimetric assays, however, related reports on the detection of sodium nitrite with the assistance of smartphones are relatively rare. The traditional method of qualitative analysis by fluorescence intensity is not very intuitive and portable, so there is a need to create a practical fluorescent test paper that can be linked to the detection of sodium nitrite using color recognition software in smartphones. Diazo coupling is a characteristic reaction of primary aromatic amines. Y-CDs can react with NO2 − to form a diazonium salt under an acidic solution. The diazonium salt and the unreacted amino Y-CDs can then participate in the coupling reaction to form an orange-red cyclic azo compound [65]. So Y-CDs can be utilized for colorimetric NO2 − detection using the diazo coupling reaction. As shown in Fig. 6 a, this is a schematic diagram of the process of detecting NaNO2 using a smartphone photo. For the Y-CDs test paper we prepared, as shown in the Fig. 6b we can clearly see the color change of the test paper under daylight. For R-CDs, as shown in the Fig. 6c, we can see that the red fluorescence of the fluorescent paper will be dimmed as the concentration of NaNO2 increases under the UV light. Finally, the finished photos were imported into the color desk of the smartphone for color parameter analysis. In order to reduce the error concerning the extracted color parameters, we performed the extraction of RGB and other parameters from the middle region of the test strip. In the Fig. 6d and 6e, a strong linear relationship was observed between the red emission/blue emission (R/G) ratio of Y-CDs paper and the brightness (L) value of R-CDs paper and between 0 and 300 µM NaNO2 concentration. In conclusion, these results demonstrate that fluorescent paper can be used to visually detect the presence of NaNO2, and can provide good ideas for future detection of NaNO2, which greatly expands its application in the field of detection.

In conclusion, we propose a strategy of synthesizing multicolor luminescent CDs by co-doping with S and N for NaNO2 detection. The emission peaks of CDs were shifted from green to red over 100 nm, which presented the strongest emissions at 520, 555 and 625 nm, respectively. The particle size of the synthesized CDs increased gradually as the emission wavelength increased from 520 to 625 nm. Based on the elemental high-resolution analysis of XPS and the corresponding PL characterization, it can be found that the tunable luminescence mechanism of the red-shift in the wavelength may account for the increased graphitic N and C = O content in the CDs. Besides, Y-CDs and R-CDs, prepared using S and N co-doping techniques, enabled highly selective and sensitive detection of NaNO2. Moreover, the detection of NaNO2 could be visualized by our test paper combined with a smartphone photo. Hence, this portable test strip holds potential for future detection of NaNO2 in food.

Xiao Gong: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Mengyi Wu: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Jiurong Li: Formal analysis. Shiwei Zhang: Formal analysis. Min Wu: Writing – review & editing, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Wu thanks the support by the Sichuan Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholars (2022JDJQ0049), and the Scientific and Technological Achievements Transformation Fund of West China Hospital, Sichuan University (Grant CGZH21002).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Revealing the tunable photoluminescence properties of graphene quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Sk, M.A.; Ananthanarayanan, A.; Huang, L.; Lim, K.H.; Chen, P. Revealing the tunable photoluminescence properties of graphene quantum dots. J. Mater. Chem. C 2014, 2 (34), 6954–6960.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Temperature- and pH-sensitive nitrogen and sulfur codoped carbon quantum dots for sequential detection of Fe3+ and H2S

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Li, Y.; Yuan, Y.; Liang, X.; Zhao, L. Temperature- and pH-sensitive nitrogen and sulfur codoped carbon quantum dots for sequential detection of Fe3+ and H2S. ACS Appl. Nano Mater. 2022, 5 (10), 14507–14519.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Oxidized nanocellulose facilitates preparing photoluminescent nitrogen-doped fluorescent carbon dots for Fe3+ ions detection and bioimaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Liu, Z.; Chen, M.; Guo, Y.; Zhou, J.; Shi, Q.; Sun, R. Oxidized nanocellulose facilitates preparing photoluminescent nitrogen-doped fluorescent carbon dots for Fe3+ ions detection and bioimaging. Chem. Eng. J. 2020, 384, 123260.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Novel processing for color-tunable luminescence carbon dots and their advantages in biological systems

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Jiao, Y.; Liu, Y.; Meng, Y.; Gao, Y.; Lu, W.; Liu, Y.; Gong, X.; Shuang, S.; Dong, C. Novel processing for color-tunable luminescence carbon dots and their advantages in biological systems. ACS Sustain. Chem. Eng. 2020, 8 (23), 8585–8592.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mechanism for excitation-dependent photoluminescence from graphene quantum dots and other graphene oxide derivates: consensus, debates and challenges

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Gan, Z.; Xu, H.; Hao, Y. Mechanism for excitation-dependent photoluminescence from graphene quantum dots and other graphene oxide derivates: consensus, debates and challenges. Nanoscale 2016, 8 (15), 7794–7807.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Concentration-dependent color tunability of nitrogen-doped carbon dots and their application for iron(III) detection and multicolor bioimaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wang, C.; Hu, T.; Wen, Z.; Zhou, J.; Wang, X.; Wu, Q.; Wang, C. Concentration-dependent color tunability of nitrogen-doped carbon dots and their application for iron(III) detection and multicolor bioimaging. J. Colloid Interface Sci. 2018, 521, 33–41.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation of panchromatic carbon dots by drug function preservation strategy and its intracellular application for cancer diagnosis and therapeutics

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Shi, J.; Ning, J.; Hu, G.; Guo, W.; Zhou, Y. Preparation of panchromatic carbon dots by drug function preservation strategy and its intracellular application for cancer diagnosis and therapeutics. Appl. Surf. Sci. 2023, 618, 156564.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Long, X.; Wang, C.; Ma, Y.; Wu, S.; Hao, L. Synthesis of multiple-color emitting carbon dots by co-doping of sulfur and nitrogen. Dyes Pigm. 2023, 209, 110886.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical synthesis of fluorescence-enhanced carbon dots with multicolor emission via surface nitrogen and sulfur modulation for information encryption applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhao, Q.; Wang, X.; Song, Q.; Zang, Z.; Fan, C.; Li, L.; Yu, X.; Lu, Z.; Zhang, X. Electrochemical synthesis of fluorescence-enhanced carbon dots with multicolor emission via surface nitrogen and sulfur modulation for information encryption applications. J. Mater. Chem. C 2023.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Li, Y.; Shu, H.; Niu, X.; Wang, J. Electronic and optical properties of edge-functionalized graphene quantum dots and the underlying mechanism. J. Phys. Chem. C 2015, 119 (44), 24950–24957.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Feng, J.; Dong, H.; Pang, B.; Shao, F.; Zhang, C.; Yu, L.; Dong, L. Theoretical study on the optical and electronic properties of graphene quantum dots doped with heteroatoms. Phys. Chem. Chem. Phys. 2018, 20 (22), 15244–15252.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Dai, R.; Chen, X.; Ouyang, N.; Hu, Y. A pH-controlled synthetic route to violet, green, and orange fluorescent carbon dots for multicolor light-emitting diodes. Chem. Eng. J. 2022, 431, 134172.

Mater. 2022, 430, 128393.

Acta Part A 2023, 301, 122947.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Deng, Y.; Zhou, Y.; Li, Q.; Qian, J. One-step hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots for high-sensitivity visual detection of nitrite and ascorbic acid. Anal. Methods 2021, 13 (33), 3685–3692.
